exemplar.title: The mathematics of a basketball trajectory

detailedExemplarData.title: The mathematics of a basketball trajectory

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear title and introduction that situates the investigation focus
Logical sequence from projectile to drag to Magnus effect
Adequate organization with introduction, body and conclusion sections
weaknesses: Abrupt transitions between sections disrupt coherence
Inconsistent formatting of table headers and units
Overly verbose aim paragraph and repeated derivations reduce conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work in band 2: it shows some coherence and an adequate structure, but repeated material, abrupt section shifts and formatting inconsistencies prevent it from reaching band 3. A score of 2 reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Correct use of vector decomposition and trigonometric notation in resolving velocities
Accurate derivation of vertical acceleration from Newtonâs second law
Use of algebraic expressions, a table and a verbal force diagram to represent ideas
weaknesses: Notation inconsistent (e.g., vây vs. v_{0v}, misuse of Ï instead of g)
Units frequently omitted in equations and final results
Abrupt context shifts and occasional explanatory digressions hinder clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical communication aligns with band 2: there is some appropriate notation and multiple representations, but inconsistency in symbols, missing units and lapses in logical flow restrict performance to level 2.

strengths: Personal choice of modeling a basketball shot shows genuine interest
Anecdotal remarks and reflection sections reveal a personal voice
weaknesses: Mathematics follows standard textbook derivations with little original insight
Limited independent thinking beyond standard models
Extensions (drag, Magnus effect) adapted from sources rather than devised independently
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Personal engagement is limited (band 1): the topic reflects personal interest, but independent thinking and creativity are modest. A score of 1 accurately represents this level of engagement.

strengths: Student identifies model limitations and suggests possible extensions
Brief comments on personal learning and connection to future practice
weaknesses: No in-depth analysis of why results differ or what they imply
Lack of quantitative evaluation (error bounds, data comparison)
Reflection is superficial and does not critically examine model validity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection corresponds to band 1: there is basic commentary and listing of limitations but no substantial critical analysis or comprehensive evaluation. Thus, a score of 1 is appropriate.

strengths: Relevant use of projectile motion, drag and Magnus effect throughout
Correct application of differential equations and integration for motion components
Concrete numerical examples reinforce theoretical predictions
weaknesses: Algebraic inconsistencies (typos in quadratic formula, missing decimal), use of undefined symbols
Sign errors and occasional misâtyped expressions weaken accuracy
No verification of results against empirical data or error analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall use of mathematics merits band 2: the mathematics is relevant and at course level, but significant errors and lack of verification limit the demonstration of full understanding required for band 3. A score of 2 aligns with the strand assessments.

strandSelections.reasoning: Within derivations, steps are usually shown in order, but mistakes (e.g., confusing vây with v_{0v}, disappearance of constants) detract from clarity. Explanatory sentences are present but occasionally digress into anecdote. Hence the logical communication is partial rather than good.

The student demonstrates some understanding of calculus and mechanics, but algebraic inconsistencies (e.g., mis-typed quadratic formula, sign errors, use of Ï) and absence of verification against data reveal gaps. Errors are significant yet not so pervasive as to nullify the mathematics, aligning with âsome understanding with errorsâ (band 2).

Equations, a small table and one verbal diagram of forces constitute multiple representations. Absence of graphs or technology-generated plots limits effectiveness; nevertheless, the mix of algebra, table and vector description provides âsomeâ representation, meriting band 2.

The exploration proceeds from basic projectile equations, through drag, and finally to the Magnus effect in a sequential manner that the reader can follow. However, transitions between sections are often abrupt, some derivations are repeated, and algebraic errors (e.g., missing units, inconsistent symbols) occasionally break the logical flow. These weaknesses prevent the piece from reaching the consistently coherent standard of band 3.

Including drag and Magnus effect shows willingness to extend the basic model, yet these extensions are lifted largely from secondary sources and contain conceptual slips. Thus creativity is present but limited, aligning with band 1.

The student remarks that drag reduces distance by 1.03 m or 3.357 m, but does not analyse why the discrepancy is significant or compare with empirical data. Such basic commentary constitutes only âbasic analysisâ (band 1).
Limitations and possible extensions are listed, yet no quantitative evaluation of model accuracy or error bounds is attempted. Consequently, the evaluation is limited, warranting band 1.

Reflection is confined to brief comments about personal learning and generic statements on limitations. There is little depth: no discussion of parameter sensitivity or model validity. This limited reflection fits band 1.

Standard mathematical symbols are used for vectors, trigonometric functions and integrals, but several notational slips occur (switching between subscripts x/y and v/h, misuse of arrows, and an undefined symbol Ï in one formula). The language is mostly correct yet not fully consistent, aligning with the âsome appropriateâ descriptor.

Sections headed âReflectionâ and anecdotal remarks about how the model may help future games provide a personal voice, but the overall approach (projectile motion with drag) is generic. Evidence therefore meets âsome personal approachâ rather than âclearâ or âhighly personalâ.

Most material is relevant to modelling a basketball shot, yet lengthy verbatim re-derivations of textbook projectile formulae and extended narrative remarks inflate the word count. Repetition of the same numerical example (e.g., hâ = 2 m, vâ = 10 m sâ»Â¹) without new insight shows limited concision, placing the work solidly in band 2.

Projectile motion, drag (linear and quadratic) and Magnus effect are all directly relevant to modelling basketball trajectories, and each is applied within the investigation. Although some digressions occur, the mathematics is generally pertinent, satisfying ârelevant mathematics used appropriatelyâ (band 3).

The student chooses the topic because of personal interest in basketball and decides what variables to include, indicating limited independent thinking. However, most mathematics follows standard textbook derivations, with little original insight, fitting band 1.

An introduction, body and conclusion are present and sub-headings are used. Nevertheless, headings do not always match content, and key results appear in the appendix rather than the main text. Graphs are absent, so results are not visually organised. Overall structure is adequate but not "clear" or polished.

Differential equations, integration and vector cross-products are at HL level and are applied in context, albeit not always rigorously. The work therefore shows mathematics âcommensurate with course levelâ, consistent with band 3.

annotations.comment: Table headers and entries lack consistent formatting of units, making interpretation harder.
The title and introductory statement clearly identify the investigationâs focus and provide effective context.
The aim paragraph repeats earlier points and is overly verbose; condense and avoid redundancy to improve conciseness.
Section headings are not always aligned with content; refine or reorganize headings to enhance navigability.
The explanation separating velocity into horizontal and vertical components is clear and uses correct trigonometric reasoning.
Units are omitted in the trigonometric equations; include units to reinforce dimensional consistency.
The list defining symbols omits the bullet symbolâs meaning, leading to slight ambiguity; clarify notation.
Derivation of vertical acceleration from Newtonâs second law is accurate and clearly presented.
Notation switches to v_{0v}(t) instead of v_{0y}(t), causing inconsistency; standardize subscripts.
Derivations and personal reflections are mixed; separate them to maintain logical coherence of analysis.
Use of initialâcondition application to solve for Câ is correctly executed, showing strong understanding.
Numeric example at 7 m highlights concrete model predictions and reinforces mathematical link.
Transition to new scenario variables (h_opponent, d_opponent) is abrupt; introduce context more explicitly.
Comparative scenarios effectively demonstrate the modelâs sensitivity to initial speed.
Typographical error: Ï is used instead of g in denominator; Ï is undefined here and should be corrected.
Vertical force equation neatly integrates drag, Magnus, and gravity terms, demonstrating model completeness.
Concrete calculation of Magnus force with real data effectively demonstrates practical application.
Units are omitted in final accelerations; including units would make numeric results clearer.
Integration to find x(t) is logically structured, demonstrating correct application of calculus.
Personal anecdote at the end interrupts technical flow; consider moving it to a reflection section.
Comparison of linear and quadratic drag differences is insightful and deepens understanding.
When comparing distance reductions, state approximate error bounds to clarify the significance of the differences.
Typo in the quadratic substitution: â10 Â· 0766â is missing the decimal point; correct to â10 Â· 0.766â.
Introducing linear drag through differential equation deepens the modelâs realism and mathematical depth.
Quadratic drag derivation via separation of variables is correctly executed and illustrates rigorous use.
Prior to integrating v_x(t), clarify its definition and origin to improve logical clarity.
Magnus force model introduction correctly applies vector crossâproduct notation to fluid dynamics.
The expression âC_L Ï v Ïâ appears without parentheses; adding them would clarify the multiplication grouping.
Limitations section lists constraints but lacks critical analysis of model validity; deepen evaluation.
Extension to include empirical data collection would strengthen model validation and practical relevance.
Reflection articulates personal learning and subject connection effectively, demonstrating strong engagement.
